title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
orientation: A foundational position statement explaining why Abolitionists Rising distinguishes itself from the pro-life movement, framed for Christians and pro-lifers willing to weigh anti-abortion strategy against scripture rather than against political pragmatism.
audiences: Self-identified pro-lifer reconsidering the movement's strategy
Christian pastor or church leader weighing the abolitionist critique
Abolitionist activist seeking a clear definitional statement
Skeptic or journalist trying to understand the abolitionist/pro-life distinction

questions.question: What is the core difference between an abolitionist and a pro-lifer on abortion?
questions.audience: Self-identified pro-lifer reconsidering the movement's strategy
questions.answer: The article concedes that pro-lifers and abolitionists agree abortion is bad, but argues abolitionists treat abortion as murder and seek its total, immediate criminalization rather than its regulation. The pro-life establishment, by contrast, has spent decades trying to compromise with abortion and reduce it incrementally, which the article says betrays the very claim that abortion is murder.
questions.quote: We, as an abolitionist organizations, agree with the pro-life position that abortion is bad, but further, we believe abortion is murder and ought to be treated as such. While many who call themselves pro-life agree with us that abortion is murder, abortion has not been opposed by the pro-life political establishment in a manner consistent with its being murder.

questions.question: Why do abolitionists oppose incremental pro-life legislation like heartbeat or pain-capable bills?
questions.answer: The article argues that incremental bills implicitly teach the public that abortion is acceptable until some arbitrary marker like a heartbeat or fetal pain, which entrenches the dehumanization of younger preborn humans. Instead of training the culture to see abortion as the murder of an image-bearer of God, such laws train it to draw morally arbitrary lines.
questions.quote: By writing such laws, pro-life legislators train the culture to believe that abortion is permissible up to the point of pain or the possession of a beating heart instead of instructing the culture to abolish abortion because it is the murder of a human being created in the image of God.

questions.question: What real-world examples does the article give of incrementalism backfiring?
questions.audience: Skeptic or journalist trying to understand the abolitionist/pro-life distinction
questions.answer: The article cites two anecdotes: an Ohio state senator's aide telling abolitionist James Silberman that the senator personally believed life begins at a heartbeat shortly after Ohio's Heartbeat Bill passed, and a mother outside an Oklahoma abortion facility telling T. Russell Hunter that her abortion was acceptable because the baby would not feel pain. The article attributes both views to messaging from incremental pro-life legislation.
questions.quote: A young mother who Russell was pleading with not to have the abortion told him, “It’s okay. My baby won’t even feel any pain.” Where did she learn that it’s acceptable to murder your baby as long as he or she doesn’t feel pain? From the Pro-Life Movement and the Pain-Capable Unborn Child Protection Act which highlights pain as the reason abortion should be illegal.

questions.question: How does the abolitionist movement view the role of the church in ending abortion?
questions.audience: Christian pastor or church leader weighing the abolitionist critique
questions.answer: The article identifies the apathy of the church as the most important obstacle the gospel must confront, and faults pastors and Christian leaders for deferring to pro-life strategists without testing their counsel against scripture. It claims abortion will only be abolished when the church takes its rightful place in the fight.
questions.quote: Most importantly, we bring the Gospel into conflict with the apathy of the Church. That's the most important part because it is when the Church takes its rightful place in the battle to abolish abortion that abortion will be abolished.

questions.question: If abolitionists reject compromise, do they also reject any near-term reduction of abortions?
questions.audience: Self-identified pro-lifer reconsidering the movement's strategy
questions.answer: No. The article states that while total abolition is unlikely overnight, abolitionists work to reduce abortions through moral suasion and material assistance to mothers, even as they consistently demand total and immediate criminalization. Reduction work is paired with, not substituted for, the call for abolition.
questions.quote: Knowing that abortion is not likely to be abolished overnight, the abolitionist consistently calls for total and immediate abolition while working diligently to reduce the number of abortions by practicing moral suasion and assistance for as long as it takes and until total abolition is accomplished.

questions.question: Do abolitionists believe women who have abortions should be punished?
questions.audience: Skeptic or journalist trying to understand the abolitionist/pro-life distinction
questions.answer: The article argues that the gospel offers forgiveness and restoration to repentant post-abortive mothers and fathers, but insists this does not exempt them from civil punishment. It claims there is no conflict between preaching forgiveness in Christ and applying laws against murder equally to all people, citing Isaiah, Amos, and Luke.
questions.quote: This truth does not mean that there should be no punishment for choosing to murder your child, however. There is no conflict between preaching the Gospel and establishing justice in the courts (Isaiah 1:16-17; Amos 5:15; Luke 11:42).

questions.question: When did the modern abolitionist movement begin and how does this group relate to it?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist seeking a clear definitional statement
questions.answer: The article dates the reignition of the American Abolitionist Movement to 2011 and presents the authoring organization as part of that movement. It frames the rebirth as a needed reformation of how Christians fight abortion after roughly 50 years of pro-life compromise under Roe.
questions.quote: The need for a reformation of the way Christians fight abortion was met in 2011 when the Abolitionist Movement in America was reignited. We are a part of that movement.

questions.question: How does the article ultimately define abolitionism?
questions.audience: Christian pastor or church leader weighing the abolitionist critique
questions.answer: The article defines abolitionism as nothing more than consistent biblical Christianity practiced in a culture that engages in child sacrifice. In the present moment, that means working to abolish abortion in the name of, by the power of, and for the glory of Jesus Christ.
questions.quote: Simply put, abolitionism is nothing more or less than the practice of consistent biblical Christianity in a culture that practices child sacrifice. In our context, with abortion being the evil of our age, abolitionism is about the abolition of abortion in the name of, by the power of, and for the glory of Jesus Christ.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual
related.reason: Develops the immediatism position central to this article's rejection of incremental pro-life strategy.

related.title: No Exceptions
related.reason: Expands the article's claim that abolitionists refuse to make exceptions in anti-abortion legislation.

related.title: Biblical, Not Secular
related.reason: Companion definitional page contrasting abolitionist scriptural grounding with the pro-life movement's worldly wisdom critique made here.

related.title: Equal Protection
related.reason: Articulates the equal-protection criminalization framework this article calls for.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Directly enumerates the case against the incremental bills this article critiques.

related.title: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
related.reason: Concise framing of the same incrementalism-versus-immediatism distinction the article relies on.

related.title: Abolitionist Perspective on the Texas Heartbeat Bill: Three Quick Observations
related.reason: Applies the article's heartbeat-bill critique to a specific high-profile piece of legislation.
